title: What Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation? A CRM Guide
content:
When a Phase I archaeological survey identifies a potential historic site on your project area, the work isn't finished — it's just beginning. A Phase II archaeological evaluation is the next critical step in the cultural resource management (CRM) process, determining whether that site is significant enough to warrant protection or further study under federal law.

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is a subsurface investigation that tests sites identified during Phase I survey to determine National Register of Historic Places (NRHP) eligibility. RPA-certified archaeologists excavate systematic test units, analyze artifacts and features, and issue a report recommending whether a site is eligible, not eligible, or needs additional study.

Why Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Matters for Your Project

A Phase II archaeological evaluation bridges the gap between initial site discovery and a formal regulatory decision. It answers the single most important question under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA): is this site eligible for listing on the National Register of Historic Places?

Without this determination, federal agencies cannot legally proceed with an undertaking that may affect the site. Developers, state transportation departments, and federal project sponsors all depend on Phase II results to move their timelines forward. Skipping this step — or cutting corners — can lead to costly project delays, permit denials, or regulatory violations.

The evaluation also protects project budgets. A clearly documented finding of "not eligible" allows construction to proceed without further archaeological consultation. A finding of "eligible" triggers additional planning, but with a defined scope rather than open-ended uncertainty.

Understanding what happens during a Phase II archaeological evaluation helps project managers plan timelines, manage stakeholder expectations, and ensure compliance with historic preservation requirements from the start.

What Does a Phase II Evaluation Actually Involve?

A Phase II evaluation uses controlled subsurface excavation to gather enough data about a site's content, integrity, and research potential to support an NRHP eligibility determination. The scope and methods are tailored to each site's characteristics and the research questions it may be able to answer.

The process typically begins with a research design — a written plan that defines the evaluation's goals, the site's archaeological context, and the excavation strategy. This document guides all fieldwork and ensures the investigation is defensible to reviewing agencies.

Field methods commonly include:

  • Hand-excavated test units (typically 1x1 or 1x2 meter squares) placed systematically across the site
  • Machine-assisted stripping of plowzone deposits to expose subsurface features
  • Feature documentation including mapping, photographing, and sampling pits, hearths, middens, and structural remains
  • Artifact recovery and cataloging with provenience control by level and unit
  • Soil sampling for flotation, radiocarbon dating, or other laboratory analyses

After fieldwork, laboratory analysis processes and interprets all recovered materials. The final evaluation report synthesizes these findings and presents a formal NRHP eligibility recommendation with supporting rationale.

How Is NRHP Eligibility Determined During Phase II?

NRHP eligibility is evaluated against four criteria established by the National Park Service. A site must meet at least one criterion and retain sufficient integrity to convey its significance. Phase II data is the primary evidence used to make this determination.

Criterion A applies to sites associated with broad patterns of history — a Civil War encampment or an early industrial complex, for example. Criterion B covers sites linked to important historical figures. Criterion C addresses sites significant for their architecture, design, or construction methods. Criterion D — the most commonly applied in archaeological contexts — recognizes sites that have yielded, or may yield, information important to history or prehistory.

Integrity is equally important. A site may have exceptional research potential but still be ineligible if disturbance has compromised its deposits beyond recognition. Phase II testing directly measures integrity by documenting artifact density, feature preservation, stratigraphic coherence, and the degree of post-depositional disturbance.

The lead archaeologist synthesizes all of this evidence into a written eligibility recommendation, which the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) and the federal agency then review and formally adopt or contest.

Who Conducts Phase II Evaluations and What Are the Standards?

Phase II archaeological evaluations must be conducted by qualified professionals who meet the Secretary of the Interior's Standards for Archaeology and Historic Preservation. In practice, this means the project principal investigator must hold at least a master's degree in archaeology or a closely related field, with demonstrated field experience.

Registration with the Register of Professional Archaeologists (RPA) is the recognized credential indicating that a practitioner meets these qualifications and adheres to the profession's code of conduct. When hiring a CRM firm for a Phase II evaluation, verifying RPA registration is an important due-diligence step for project sponsors.

TVAR's team of RPA-certified archaeologists brings over 14 years of experience conducting Phase II evaluations across the Southeast, including complex multicomponent sites, submerged cultural resources, and sensitive burial contexts. Our familiarity with Alabama SHPO review standards and federal agency requirements ensures evaluations are accepted the first time — not sent back for additional work.

State-specific requirements also matter. Alabama, Tennessee, Georgia, and neighboring states each have their own SHPO guidance documents and preferred formats for Phase II reports. Working with a firm that knows your state's review process reduces turnaround time significantly.

How Long Does a Phase II Evaluation Take and What Does It Cost?

A Phase II archaeological evaluation timeline depends on site size, deposit complexity, the number of test units required, and laboratory workload. Most evaluations for moderately sized sites take four to eight weeks from field mobilization to final report delivery, though complex projects can run longer.

Cost drivers include the number of field days required, laboratory processing needs (especially if flotation samples or radiocarbon dating are involved), and report production. GIS-based spatial analysis and remote sensing integration can add depth to an evaluation while sometimes reducing unnecessary fieldwork by targeting excavations more precisely.

Project sponsors should budget for Phase II early in the project development cycle. Discovering a significant site during construction is far more expensive than conducting a proper evaluation beforehand. Many federal and state transportation programs build Phase II costs into project development budgets as a standard line item.

Requesting a detailed scope of work and fee estimate from your CRM contractor before fieldwork begins protects both parties and ensures the evaluation meets regulatory requirements without unexpected overruns. Visit our research services page to learn more about our evaluation process.

Frequently Asked Questions

What triggers the need for a Phase II archaeological evaluation?

A Phase II evaluation is triggered when a Phase I survey identifies a site that cannot be determined eligible or not eligible for the NRHP based on surface evidence alone. The federal agency or SHPO determines that additional subsurface data is needed before the Section 106 process can continue.

Can a project proceed while Phase II evaluation is ongoing?

Generally, ground-disturbing work in the area of potential effect must pause until the evaluation is complete and a formal eligibility determination is made. Project sponsors can often sequence construction to avoid the site boundary while evaluation fieldwork proceeds, minimizing overall schedule impact.

What happens after a Phase II evaluation finds a site eligible?

If a site is determined eligible for the NRHP, the federal agency must consult with the SHPO and other parties to resolve adverse effects. Options include project redesign to avoid the site, archaeological data recovery (Phase III excavation), or a programmatic agreement documenting agreed-upon mitigation measures.

How is Phase II different from Phase I and Phase III archaeology?

Phase I identifies and locates sites through surface survey and shovel testing. Phase II evaluates whether identified sites are NRHP-eligible through systematic testing. Phase III — data recovery — is large-scale excavation conducted when a significant site cannot be avoided and must be documented before destruction.

Does Phase II evaluation always require excavation?

Excavation is the standard method, but non-invasive techniques such as ground-penetrating radar, magnetometry, and LiDAR analysis can supplement or, in limited cases, substitute for traditional test units when site conditions warrant and the reviewing agency concurs with the alternative approach.

Move Your Project Forward with a Qualified Phase II Team

A well-executed Phase II archaeological evaluation delivers a clear, defensible eligibility determination that keeps your project on schedule and in compliance with federal historic preservation law. The quality of the field methods, laboratory analysis, and final report directly affects how quickly reviewing agencies respond — and whether your project can proceed without additional study.

Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research (TVAR) provides comprehensive Phase II evaluation services for federal agencies, state DOTs, private developers, and utility companies throughout the Southeast. Our RPA-certified team delivers thorough, agency-ready reports grounded in rigorous fieldwork and 14+ years of regional expertise.

Ready to discuss your project's archaeological compliance needs? Contact TVAR today to speak with a qualified archaeologist about scoping a Phase II evaluation for your site.
excerpt: Learn what a Phase II archaeological evaluation involves, how NRHP eligibility is determined, and what project sponsors need to know about CRM compliance under Section 106.
